4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

In modern web architecture, it’s common to split applications across multiple servers for scalability, security, and maintainability. A frequent requirement is:

• Application 1 hosted on Server A

• Application 2 hosted on Server B

• Both accessible through the same domain
For example:
https://example.com/test1
https://example.com/test2Many developers initially assume this can be achieved through DNS. However, DNS only resolves domains and subdomains — it does not understand URL paths or subfolders.
In this article, we’ll explore how to host multiple applications on different servers while serving them under the same domain using path-based routing and reverse proxies.

Understanding the Limitation of DNS
DNS can route:
example.com
api.example.com
app.example.comBut DNS cannot route:
example.com/test1
example.com/test2This is because /test1 and /test2 are URL paths that are processed after DNS resolution has already occurred.
When a user visits:
https://example.com/test1The browser first resolves:
example.comto an IP address. Only after connecting to the server does it send the /test1 path.
Therefore, routing based on URL paths must be handled by a web server, reverse proxy, or load balancer.

Recommended Architecture
Consider the following setup:
Domain:
example.comServer A:
Application 1
Server B:
Application 2
Desired URLs:
example.com/test1 → Application 1
example.com/test2 → Application 2Architecture:
+----------------+
| example.com |
+--------+-------+
|
|
Reverse Proxy
|
+-------------+-------------+
| |
| |
/test1 requests /test2 requests
| |
v v
+---------------+ +---------------+
| Server A | | Server B |
| Application 1 | | Application 2 |
+---------------+ +---------------+The reverse proxy acts as a traffic controller, forwarding requests to the appropriate backend server based on the URL path.

Solution 1: Nginx Reverse Proxy
Nginx is one of the most popular solutions for path-based routing.
Assume:
Server A: 10.0.1.10
Server B: 10.0.2.20Nginx configuration:
server {
listen 80;
server_name example.com; location /test1/ {
proxy_pass http://10.0.1.10/;
proxy_set_header Host $host;
proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
}
location /test2/ {
proxy_pass http://10.0.2.20/;
proxy_set_header Host $host;
proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
}
}
With this setup:
example.com/test1 → Server A
example.com/test2 → Server BUsers see a single domain while applications remain hosted independently.

Solution 2: AWS Application Load Balancer
If you’re hosting on AWS, an Application Load Balancer (ALB) provides native path-based routing.
You can configure routing rules such as:

• /test1/* → EC2 Instance A (or Target Group A)

• /test2/* → EC2 Instance B (or Target Group B)
With these rules in place:
https://example.com/test1 → EC2 Instance A
https://example.com/test2 → EC2 Instance BBenefits

• Managed infrastructure

• SSL integration

• Auto Scaling support

• Health checks

• High availability
This approach is ideal for production-grade environments.

Solution 3: CloudFront + Multiple Origins
For applications already using a CDN, AWS CloudFront can route requests to different origins.
Example:
/test1/* → Origin A
/test2/* → Origin BAdvantages:

• Global edge caching

• Reduced latency

• DDoS protection

• Seamless SSL management
This is especially useful for geographically distributed users.

Application Configuration Considerations
Routing traffic is only half the solution.
Applications must also be aware that they are running inside a subfolder.
Laravel
Update .env:
APP_URL=https://example.com/test1Additionally:
URL::forceRootUrl(config('app.url'));Ensure all generated URLs and assets include the /test1 prefix.

React
Configure:
{
"homepage": "/test1"
}Or:
<BrowserRouter basename="/test1">
Angular
Update:
ng build --base-href=/test1/
Vue.js
Configure:
base: '/test1/'
Common Challenges
Asset Loading Issues
Incorrect:
https://example.com/css/app.cssCorrect:
https://example.com/test1/css/app.cssRedirect Loops
Applications often redirect users to:
https://example.com/logininstead of:
https://example.com/test1/loginEnsure application base URLs are configured correctly.
Session and Cookie Conflicts
If multiple applications use the same cookie names:
session
csrf_tokenthey may overwrite each other.
Use unique cookie names for each application.

Should You Use Subfolders or Subdomains?
Subfolders
example.com/test1
example.com/test2Pros

• Single domain

• Shared SEO authority

• Unified branding
Cons

• More complex application configuration

• Asset path management

• Routing challenges

Subdomains
test1.example.com
test2.example.comPros

• Easier deployment

• Minimal application changes

• Cleaner architecture
Cons

• Separate DNS records

• SEO authority split

Best Practice Recommendation
For most enterprise deployments:

• Use AWS ALB or Nginx Reverse Proxy for path-based routing.

• Ensure applications are configured with the correct base URL.

• Use SSL termination at the proxy layer.

• Monitor backend health and performance.

• Prefer subdomains if application independence is more important than a unified URL structure.

Conclusion
Hosting multiple applications on different servers under the same domain is entirely achievable, but it cannot be done through DNS alone. The correct approach is to use a reverse proxy, load balancer, or CDN-based path routing solution that forwards requests based on URL paths.
Whether you’re running Laravel, React, Angular, Vue, or microservices-based architectures, path-based routing enables a seamless user experience while maintaining independent application deployments behind the scenes.
By combining a reverse proxy with proper application configuration, you can successfully serve multiple applications under a single domain such as:
https://example.com/test1
https://example.com/test2without exposing the complexity of your backend infrastructure.
